toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
# Try downloading debug symbols from Microsoft's symbol server
# BuildId: 1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be
BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be"

# Try the ELF buildid format on MS symbol server
curl -sL -o /tmp/libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg -w "%{http_code}" \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug"
arguments.description: Download debug symbols from MS symbol server

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Download debug symbols from MS symbol server

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
# Also try downloading the library itself (with embedded symbols)
BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be"
curl -sL -o /tmp/libmonosgen-2.0.so -w "%{http_code}" \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so"
arguments.description: Download library from MS symbol server

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Download library from MS symbol server

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames in this Android tombstone. The key library is `libmonosgen-2.0.so` with BuildId `1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be`. The frames are at offsets:

- #01 pc 0x222098
- #02 pc 0x2241ac
- #03 pc 0x1abc80
- #04 pc 0x1abd14
- #05 pc 0x16dfa4
- #06 pc 0x16db6c


- #07 pc 0x16de04

To resolve these addresses to function names, I'd need the debug symbols for this specific build of the Mono runtime library. Microsoft publishes these symbols on their symbol server, so I should be able to fetch them using the BuildId to locate the correct version. So the BuildId formatting matters when constructing the URL—it might need the first byte pair swapped or kept as-is. I should try a few different approaches: downloading directly from Microsoft's symbol server, using debuginfod, or leveraging tools like llvm-symbolizer or addr2line if I can obtain the symbols. Let me first see what tools are actually available and whether I can work around the missing debug symbols. of those tools to symbolicate the addresses. Let me start by checking what's available on the system and then attempt to download the symbols.

arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line aarch64-linux-gnu-addr2line llvm-objdump 2>/dev/null; dpkg -l | grep -i llvm 2>/dev/null | head -5

data.content:
Here's the symbolicated backtrace for the `libmonosgen-2.0.so` frames:

```
#00 pc 0x06ae1c  libc.so                abort+164
#01 pc 0x222098  libmonosgen-2.0.so     ves_icall_System_Environment_FailFast        (icall.c:6244)
#02 pc 0x2241ac  libmonosgen-2.0.so     ves_icall_System_Environment_FailFast_raw    (icall-def.h:202)
#03 pc 0x1abc80  libmonosgen-2.0.so     do_icall                                     (interp.c:2457)
#04 pc 0x1abd14  libmonosgen-2.0.so     get_interp_to_native_trampoline              (interp.c:1732)
#05 pc 0x16dfa4  libmonosgen-2.0.so     handle_isinst                                (type-checking.c:?)
#06 pc 0x16db6c  libmonosgen-2.0.so     handle_isinst                                (type-checking.c:506)
#07 pc 0x16de04  libmonosgen-2.0.so     handle_isinst                                (type-checking.c:528)
#08 pc 0x00307c  <anonymous>            (managed/JIT code)
```

**Summary:** The app called `Environment.FailFast()` with abort message *"not combatible"* (visible at line 12). The call chain goes through Mono's interpreter (`do_icall` → `ves_icall_System_Environment_FailFast`) which called libc `abort()`. The bottom frames show `handle_isinst` (type-checking), and frame #08 is managed/.NET JIT code (which would need a managed stack trace to resolve further).
